concerns.text: The background model strategy ('Peak Regions from *other* cell types') is methodologically unsound for identifying cell-type-specific signals. If a TF motif is generally enriched in open chromatin across all cell types, comparing one cell type against the union of others will yield non-significant results (false negatives) because the background contains the signal. A valid design requires a matched genomic background (e.g., random regions with similar GC content and mappability) to establish a null distribution of motif occurrence, against which cell-type-specific enrichment is tested.

The validation threshold of '≥ 60% overlap' for ChIP-seq peaks is arbitrary and likely unachievable for ATAC-seq derived motif predictions. ATAC-seq identifies accessible regions, while ChIP-seq identifies specific TF binding events; not all accessible regions contain the TF, and ChIP-seq peaks often extend beyond the core motif. A 60% overlap threshold risks classifying biologically valid, lower-specificity predictions as failures, invalidating the 'mechanism' claim. The metric should be enrichment of ChIP-seq peaks over the background, not raw overlap percentage.

The plan assumes FIMO/HOMER can run on 2 cores within 6 hours for ~100k peaks across 5 cell types against the full JASPAR database. FIMO is computationally intensive (sequence scanning). Without explicit sampling strategies or a defined subset of motifs to scan, this resource constraint may force the pipeline to timeout or fail, preventing the completion of the analysis required to answer the research question.
